content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 95814-2329 
Matthew R. Feaster 
10132 Sheffield Oak Way 
Elk Grove, CA 95624 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
July 15, 201 l 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-11-062 
Dear Mr. Feaster: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 However, because your question presents issues requiring a 
policy interpretation best made by the Commission, we decline to provide formal assistance 
pursuant to Regulation 18329(b)(8)(E).2 .• 
QUESTION 
Are donations including cash payments, gift baskets, and the use of a restaurant for 
fundraising events, provided by close family friends and members of the church you attend, to 
assist you in an effort to adopt a child, gifts under the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
Your question presents an issue requiring a policy interpretation best left to the 
Commission. It is anticipated that Commission staff will present a regulatory proposal to the 
Commission in the near future. 
FACTS 
You are an Administrator I with the Franchise Tax Board and have held this position 
since April 5, 2010. As designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code, your position 
requires the full disclosure of all sources of gifts. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114, Regulation I 8329 (c)(3).) 


File No. I-11-062 
Page No. 2 
You and your wife are in the process of adopting an orphaned child from Ethiopia. This 
is an expensive process and costs typically range from $25,000 to $30,000. In light of these 
costs, you have had many family members,3 members of your church, and close family friends 
who desired to assist you in the process. You stated that their assistance was greatly appreciated 
and accepted. At this time, you and your wife have received monetary donations from many 
sources including amounts exceeding both $50 and $420. 
In addition to monetary donations, a long-time family friend who owns a local restaurant 
has hosted three fundraising events for your benefit at his restaurant. During these events, 
friends and family gathered to dine at the restaurant and the restaurant donated a portion of its 
proceeds for your assistance. The final amount you received from each of the three events 
exceeded $420. Donated gift baskets were also raffled off during these events. 
While you cannot provide a full accounting of the sources of the donations you have 
received, in a telephone conversation, you stated that you have attempted to report the receipt of 
the donations as gifts to the best of your knowledge. You also stated that any assistance of $50 
or more came from a family member, a member of your church, or a close family friend. 
ANALYSIS 
The term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as: 
"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to 
the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and 
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the 
rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of 
the public without regard to official status. "4 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials,5 the Act regulates the 
receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways: 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $420 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 
18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all elected state and local officials or other individuals 
designated in Section 87200; all candidates for state, local, or judicial office; and any employee 
3 Gifts from certain family members do not constitute gifts under the Act and are not reportable. As 
provided by Regulation 18942, this exception is limited to gifts "from an individual's spouse, child, parent, 
grandparent, grandchild, brothe1', sister, parent-in-law, brother-in-law, sister-in-law, nephew, niece, aunt, uncle, or 
• 
first cousin or the spouse of any such person .... " 
4 Section 82044 defines payment, in part, as any "rendering of ... services or anything else of value, 
whether tangible or intangible." 
5 A "public official " is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local governmental 
agency." (Section 82048.) 


File No. 1-11-062 
Page No. 3 
designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code, as adopted pursuant to Section 87300, 
if the employee would be required to disclose the receipt of income or gifts from the source of 
the gift on his or her statement of economic interest. (Section 89503.) 
Secondly, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift ( or any gifts that 
aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year are disclosed 
on the officials' statements of economic interests. Reporting requirements apply to all officials 
listed in Section 87200 (Section 87202), all candidates for an office specified in Section 87200 
(Section 87201), and employees designated in an agency's conflict-of-interest code as specified 
in the code (Section 87302(b)). 
Finally, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the 
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $420 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. (Sections 
87100, 87103(e), Regulations 18700, 18703.4.) 
Notwithstanding these general rules, your question involves complex questions of 
interpretation regarding the Act's gift rules as applied to donations made under circumstances 
strongly indicating a charitable purpose, wholly unrelated to your position as a governmental 
official. Accordingly, your question presents issues requiring a policy interpretation best left to 
the Commission. It is anticipated that Commission staff will present a regulatory proposal to the 
Commission addressing these issues before the end of the year. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
John W. Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel 
-;;}-
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Counsel, Legal Division

assist you in an effort to adopt a child, gifts under the Act?

Commission in the near future.

QUESTION: Are donations including cash payments, gift baskets, and the use of a restaurant for 
fundraising events, provided by close family friends and members of the church you attend, to 
assist you in an effort to adopt a child, gifts under the Act?

CONCLUSION: Your question presents an issue requiring a policy interpretation best left to the 
Commission. It is anticipated that Commission staff will present a regulatory proposal to the 
Commission in the near future.
